description:
<title>Schedule Periodic Connectivity-Check with a User Cron Job</title>

    <description>
        You are a network engineer who suspects that a workstation occasionally
        loses external connectivity.  
        Create **one** user-level cron entry that automatically runs every
        minute and appends the result of a single-packet ICMP ping to a log
        file located in your home directory.

        1. The cron entry must:
           • Execute the command  
             ping -c1 -W 2 8.8.8.8  
             (that is: send one packet, wait two seconds for a reply).  
           • Redirect both STDOUT and STDERR (»2>&1«) to the file  
             /home/user/connectivity.log  
             in append mode (»>>«).
        2. The schedule specification must be *every minute* (i.e. the five
           leading asterisks: “* * * * *”).
        3. Place the entry in the *user* crontab (the one managed by the plain
           “crontab” command, **not** /etc/crontab or /etc/cron.d/).
        4. There must be exactly **one** functional line in the user crontab
           after you are done (comments are allowed but will be ignored by the
           automated check).
        5. Do **not** create the log file manually—the first cron run will do
           that.  Your task is to install the cron job only.

        The automated grader will verify success by running
           crontab -l
        in the container.  
        The output **must** contain a single uncommented line that matches this
        pattern *exactly* (excluding leading/trailing whitespace):

            * * * * * ping -c1 -W 2 8.8.8.8 >> /home/user/connectivity.log 2>&1

        Anything else (different target IP, wrong redirect syntax, wrong
        schedule, etc.) will be marked as incorrect.  Make sure no extra spaces
        appear in the middle of the command.
    </description>

    <acceptance_criteria>
        1. crontab -l returns precisely the line above (comments ignored).
        2. The log file path is /home/user/connectivity.log (even if empty).
    </acceptance_criteria>

    <hints>
        • You can replace the entire crontab in one shot using echo/printf and
          a pipe.  
        • Remember that a user-level cron job runs with your UID, so you can
          safely write to /home/user.  
        • If you need to re-run your command, use “crontab -r” to clear the
          crontab and start over.
    </hints>
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth values that the automated test framework will check -->
    <expected_cron_line>* * * * * ping -c1 -W 2 8.8.8.8 >> /home/user/connectivity.log 2>&1</expected_cron_line>
name: task_000000_39a72386
